n110011: tn Heb “was old, coming into the days” (i.e., advancing in years).
n110014: tn Heb “let them seek for my master, the king, a young girl, a virgin.” The third person plural subject of the verb is indefinite (see GKC 460 §144.f). The appositional expression, “a young girl, a virgin,” is idiomatic; the second term specifically defines the more general first term (see IBHS 230 §12.3b).
n110015: tn Heb “and she will stand before the king.” The Hebrew phrase “stand before” can mean “to attend; to serve” (BDB 764 s.v. ‏עָמַד‎).
n110016: tn Heb “and she will lie down in your bosom.” The expression might imply sexual intimacy (see 2 Sam 12:3 [where the lamb symbolizes Bathsheba] and Mic 7:5), though v. 4b indicates that David did not actually have sex with the young woman.
n110017: tn Heb “and my master, the king, will be warm.”
n110019: tn Heb “did not know her.” The verb ‏יָדַע‎ (yadaʿ, “to know”) is a euphemism for sexual relations.
n1100110:
tn Heb “son of Haggith,” but since this formula usually designates the father (who in this case was David), the translation specifies that David was Adonijah’s father.
sn Haggith was one of David’s wives (2 Sam 3:4; 2 Chr 3:2).
n1100116: tn Heb “did not correct him from his days.” The phrase “from his days” means “from his earliest days,” or “ever in his life.” See GKC 382 §119.w, n. 2.
n1100117: tn Heb “and she gave birth to him after Absalom.” This does not imply they had the same mother; Absalom’s mother was Maacah, not Haggith (2 Sam 3:4).
n1100120: tn Heb “Adonijah.” The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun (“him”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1100123: tc The ancient Greek version omits this appositional phrase.
n1100126: tn Heb “and our master David does not know.”
n1100127: tn Heb “now, come.” The imperative of ‏הָלַךְ‎ (halakh) is here used as an introductory interjection. See BDB 234 s.v. ‏חָלַךְ‎.
n1100129: tn Heb “come, go to.” The imperative of ‏הָלַךְ‎ (halakh) is here used as an introductory interjection. See BDB 234 s.v. ‏חָלַךְ‎.
n1100131: tn In the Hebrew text the sentence is introduced by the particle ‏הִנֵּה‎ (hinneh, “look”), which here draws attention to Nathan’s concluding word of assurance and support. For this use of the word, see HALOT 252 s.v. ‏הִנֵּה‎.
n1100132: tc The Hebrew text reads, “I will come after you.”
n1100136: tc Instead of ‏עַתָּה‎ (ʿattah, “now”) many Hebrew mss, along with the Old Greek, Syriac Peshitta, and Latin Vulgate, have the similar sounding independent pronoun ‏אַתָּה‎ (ʾattah, “you”). This reading is followed in the present translation.
n1100138: tc Many Hebrew mss have ‏עַתָּה‎ (ʿattah, “now”) rather than the similar sounding independent pronoun ‏אַתָּה‎ (ʾattah, “you”).
n1100139: tn Heb “the eyes of all Israel are upon you to declare to them who will sit on the throne of my master the king after him.”
n1100140: tn The words “if a decision is not made” are added for clarification.
n1100142: tn Heb “I and my son Solomon.” The order has been reversed in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1100143: tn Heb “will be guilty”; NASB “considered offenders”; TEV “treated as traitors.”
n1100144: tn Heb “look.” The particle ‏הִנֵּה‎ (hinneh) here draws attention to Nathan’s arrival and invites the audience to view the scene through the eyes of the participants.
n1100145: tn Heb “ground.” Since this was indoors, “floor” is more appropriate than “ground.”
n1100149: tc Many Hebrew mss and ancient textual witnesses agree with the Qere in reading this as singular, “your servant.”
n1100150: tn Heb “From my master the king is this thing done, and you did not make known to your servants who will sit on the throne of my master the king after him?”
n1100152: sn Summon Bathsheba. Bathsheba must have left the room when Nathan arrived (see 1:22).
n1100153: tn Heb “she came before the king and stood before the king.”
n1100156: tn Heb “bowed low, face [to] the ground, and bowed down to the king.”
n1100157: sn Summon…Nathan. Nathan must have left the room when Bathsheba reentered.
n1100159: tn The plural form is used in the Hebrew text to indicate honor and authority.
n1100160: tn Heb “mount Solomon my son on the mule that belongs to me and take him down to Gihon.”
n1100165: tn Heb “So may the Lord God of my master the king say.”
n1100166: tn Heb “and may he make his throne greater than the throne of my master King David.”
n1100167: sn The Kerethites and Pelethites were members of David’s royal guard (see 2 Sam 8:18). The Kerethites may have been descendants of an ethnic group originating in Crete.
n1100168:
tn Heb “the horn of oil.” This has been specified as olive oil in the translation for clarity.
sn A horn filled with oil. An animal’s horn was used as an oil flask in the anointing ceremony.
n1100170: tn Heb “and all the people went up after him, and the people were playing flutes and rejoicing with great joy and the ground split open at the sound of them.” The verb ‏בָּקַע‎ (baqaʿ, “to split open”), which elsewhere describes the effects of an earthquake, is obviously here an exaggeration for the sake of emphasis.
n1100171: tn Heb “And Adonijah and all the guests who were with him heard, now they had finished eating.”
n1100173: tn The Hebrew text has “look” at this point. The particle ‏הִנֵּה‎ (hinneh), “look” draws attention to Jonathan’s arrival and invites the audience to view the scene through the eyes of the participants.
n1100175: tn Heb “you are a man of strength [or “ability”] and you bring a message [that is] good.” Another option is to understand the phrase ‏אִישׁ חַיִל‎ (ʾish khayil) in the sense of “a worthy man,” that is “loyal.” See also 1 Kgs 1:52 and HALOT 311 s.v. ‏חַיִל‎.
n1100177: tn For a similar use of ‏אֲבָל‎ (ʾaval), see Gen 17:19, where God rejects Abraham’s proposal and offers an alternative.
n1100178: tn The plural form is used in the Hebrew text to indicate honor and authority.
n1100179: tn I.e., designated by anointing with oil.
n1100180: tn Heb “And also Solomon sits on the throne of the kingdom.”
n1100182: tn The plural form is used in the Hebrew text to indicate honor and authority.
n1100183: tc Many Hebrew mss agree with the Qere in reading simply “God.”
n1100184: tn Heb “make the name of Solomon better than your name, and make his throne greater than your throne.” The term ‏שֵׁם‎ (shem, “name”) is used here of one’s fame and reputation.
n1100186: tn The Hebrew text reads, “and the king said.”
n1100187: tn Or “Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, who….” In this blessing formula ‏אֲשֶׁר‎ (ʾasher, “who; because”) introduces the reason why the one being blessed deserves the honor.
n1100190: sn Grabbed hold of the horns of the altar. The “horns” of the altar were the horn-shaped projections on the four corners of the altar (see Exod 27:2). By going to the holy place and grabbing hold of the horns of the altar, Adonijah was seeking asylum from Solomon.
n1100191: tn Heb “King Solomon.” The name and title have been replaced by the pronoun (“you”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1100193: tn Heb “if he is a man of strength [or ability].” In this context, where Adonijah calls himself a “servant,” implying allegiance to the new king, the phrase ‏אִישׁ חַיִל‎ (ʾish khayil) probably carries the sense of “a worthy man,” that is, “loyal” (see HALOT 311 s.v. ‏חַיִל‎).
n1100194: tn Heb “but if evil is found in him.”
n110021: tn Heb “and the days of David approached to die.”
n110023: tn Heb “going the way of all the earth.”
n110024: tn Heb “keep the charge of the Lord your God.”
n110027: tn Heb “then you will cause to succeed all which you do and all which you turn there.”
n110028: tn Heb “then the Lord will establish his word which he spoke to me, saying.”
n1100213: tn Heb “there will not be cut off from you a man from upon the throne of Israel.”
n1100214: tn Heb “what he did to the two commanders…and he killed them.”
n1100215: tn Heb “he shed the blood of battle in peace.”
n1100216: tn Heb “and he shed the blood of battle on his belt which is on his waist and on his sandal[s] which are on his feet.” That is, he covered himself with guilt and his guilt was obvious to all who saw him.
n1100218: tn Heb “and do not bring down his grey hair in peace [to] Sheol.”
n1100220: tn Heb “and let them be among the ones who eat [at] your table.”
n1100223: tn Heb “and he cursed me with a horrible curse on the day I went to Mahanaim.”
n1100226: tc The Lucianic recension of the Old Greek and the Vulgate have here “you” rather than “now.” The two words are homonyms in Hebrew.
n1100228: tn Heb “bring his grey hair down in blood [to] Sheol.”
n1100229: tn Heb “and David lay down with his fathers.”
n1100230: sn The phrase the City of David refers here to the fortress of Zion in Jerusalem, not to Bethlehem. See 2 Sam 5:7.
n1100236: tn Heb “set their face to me to be king.”
n1100237: tn Heb “and the kingdom turned about and became my brother’s, for from the Lord it became his.”
n1100240: tn Heb “Say to Solomon the king, for he will not turn back your face, that he might give to me Abishag the Shunammite for a wife.”
n1100243: tn Heb “he set up a throne for the mother of the king.”
n1100244: tn Or “I’d like to make just one request of you.”
n1100248: tn Heb “So may God do to me, and so may he add.”
n1100249: tn Heb “if with his life Adonijah has not spoken this word.”
n1100251: tn The Hebrew text adds, “by the hand of.”
n1100252: tn Heb “and he struck him and he died.”
n1100254: tn Heb “you are a man of death,” an idiom.
n1100255: tn Heb “and because you suffered through all which my father suffered.”
n1100257: tn Heb “Joab.” The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun (“he”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1100258: sn Grabbed hold of the horns of the altar. The “horns” of the altar were the horn-shaped projections on the four corners of the altar (see Exod 27:2). By going to the holy place and grabbing hold of the horns of the altar, Joab was seeking asylum from Solomon.
n1100259: tn Heb “and it was related to King Solomon.”
n1100260: tn Heb “so Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada, saying.”
n1100261: tn Heb “saying, “In this way Joab spoke and in this way he answered me.”
n1100263: tn Heb “take away the undeserved bloodshed which Joab spilled from upon me and from upon the house of my father.”
n1100264: tn Heb “The Lord will cause his blood to return upon his head.”
n1100265: tn Heb “because he struck down two men more innocent and better than he and he killed them with the sword, and my father David did not know.”
n1100268: tn Heb “struck him and killed him.” The referent (Joab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1100270: tc The Old Greek translation includes after v. 35 some fourteen verses that are absent from the MT.
n1100273: tn Heb “and you may not go out from there here or there.”
n1100274: tn Heb “your blood will be upon your head.”
n1100275: tn Heb “Good is the word, as my master the king has spoken.”
n1100279: tn Heb “Is it not [true]…?” In the Hebrew text the statement is interrogative; the rhetorical question expects the answer, “Of course it is.”
n1100281: tn Heb “good is the word; I have heard.”
n1100282: tn Heb “Why have you not kept the oath [to] the Lord and the commandment I commanded you?”
n1100283: tn Heb “You know all the evil, for your heart knows, which you did to David my father.”
n1100284: tn Heb “The Lord will cause your evil to return upon your head.”
n1100287: tn “The king commanded Benaiah son of Jehoiada and he went out and struck him down and he died.”
n1100288: tn “And the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon.”
n110031: sn The phrase City of David refers here to the fortress of Zion in Jerusalem, not to Bethlehem. See 2 Sam 5:7.
n110033: sn Offering sacrifices at the high places. The “high places” were places of worship that were naturally or artificially elevated.
n110034: tn Heb “for the name of the Lord.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor (thus the translation here, “to honor the Lord”). The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.
n110037: tn Heb “for it was the great high place.”
n110038: tn Or, “customarily offered up.” The verb form is an imperfect, which is probably used here in a customary sense to indicate continued or repeated action in past time. See GKC 314 §107.b.
n1100313: tn Heb “in faithfulness and in innocence and in uprightness of heart with you.”
n1100314: tn Heb “and you have kept to him this great loyalty and you gave to him a son [who] sits on his throne as this day.”
n1100315: tn Heb “and I do not know going out or coming in.”
n1100316: tn There is no verb expressed in the Hebrew text; “stands” is supplied in the translation for clarification.
n1100318: tn Heb “a hearing heart.” (The Hebrew term translated “heart” often refers to the mental faculties.)
n1100321: tn Heb “for”; the word “otherwise” is used to reflect the logical sense of the statement.
n1100322: tn Heb “who is able?” The rhetorical question anticipates the answer, “no one.”
n1100325: tn The Hebrew term translated “Lord” here and in v.15 is ‏אֲדֹנָי‎ (ʾadonay).
n1100326: tn Heb “And the thing was good in the eyes of the Lord, for Solomon asked for this thing.”
n1100327: tn Heb “because you asked for this thing, and did not ask for yourself many days and did not ask for yourself riches and did not ask for the life of your enemies, but you asked for yourself understanding to hear judgment.”
n1100328: tn This statement is introduced in the Hebrew text by the particle ‏הִנֵּה‎ (hinneh, “look”) which draws attention to and emphasizes what follows.
n1100329: tn Heb “I am doing according to your words.” The perfect tense is sometimes used of actions occurring at the same time a statement is made.
n1100330: tn This statement is introduced by the particle ‏הִנֵּה‎ (hinneh, “look”) which draws attention to and emphasizes what follows. The translation assumes that the perfect tense here indicates that the action occurs as the statement is made (i.e., “right now I give you”).
n1100331: tn Heb “heart.” (The Hebrew term translated “heart” often refers to the mental faculties.)
n1100332: tn Heb “so that there has not been one like you prior to you, and after you one will not arise like you.”
n1100333: tn The translation assumes that the perfect tense here indicates that the action occurs as the statement is made.
n1100334: tn Heb “so that there is not one among the kings like you all your days.” The LXX lacks the words “all your days.”
n1100340: tn Or “tokens of peace”; NIV, TEV “fellowship offerings.”
n1100341: sn There was no one else in the house except the two of us. In other words, there were no other witnesses to the births who could identify which child belonged to which mother.
n1100345: tn Heb “look, it was not my son to whom I had given birth.”
n1100346: tn Heb “they spoke before the king.” Another option is to translate, “they argued before the king.”
n1100348: tn Heb “for her compassions grew warm for her son.”
n1100349: tn The infinitive absolute before the negated jussive emphasizes the main verb.
n1100350: tn Heb “feared,” perhaps in the sense, “stood in awe of.”
n1100352: tn Heb “the wisdom of God was in his midst for performing justice.” The phrase “wisdom of God” may be taken as an attributive genitive, “divine wisdom,” or as a genitive of source, “wisdom from God.” Even in English they are basically the same, since wisdom from God is divine in character.
n110041: tn Heb “were scribes”; NASB, NIV, NRSV “secretaries”; TEV, NLT “court secretaries.”
n110044: tn Heb “close associate of”; KJV, ASV, NASB “the king’s friend” (a title for an adviser, not just an acquaintance).
n110047: sn The work crews. This Hebrew word (‏מַס‎, mas) refers to a group of laborers conscripted for royal or public service.
n110049: sn Beginning with 4:21, the verse numbers through 5:18 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 4:21 ET = 5:1 HT, 4:22 ET = 5:2 HT, etc., through 5:18 ET = 5:32 HT. Beginning with 6:1 the numbering of verses in the English Bible and the Hebrew text is again the same.
n1100410: tn Heb “the River” (also in v. 24). This is the standard designation for the Euphrates River in biblical Hebrew.
n1100411: tn Heb “[They] were bringing tribute and were serving Solomon all the days of his life.”
n1100412: tn Heb “the food of Solomon for each day was.”
n1100413: tn As a unit of dry measure a cor was roughly equivalent to six bushels.
n1100414: tn The words “in the stall” are added for clarification; note the immediately following reference to cattle from the pasture.
n1100415: tn Heb “because.” The words “his royal court was so large” are added to facilitate the logical connection with the preceding verse.
n1100416: sn Tiphsah. This was located on the Euphrates River.
n1100417: tn Heb “for he was ruling over all [the region] beyond the River, from Tiphsah to Gaza, over all the kingdoms beyond the River, and he had peace on every side all around.”
n1100418: tn Heb “Judah and Israel lived securely, each one under his vine and under his fig tree, from Dan to Beer Sheba, all the days of Solomon.”
n1100419: tn The Hebrew text has “40,000,” but this is probably an inflated number (nevertheless it is followed by KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV, TEV, CEV). Some Greek mss of the OT and the parallel in 2 Chr 9:25 read “4,000” (cf. NAB, NIV, NCV, NLT).
n1100420: tn Heb “everyone who drew near to the table of King Solomon.”
n1100421: tn Heb “barley and straw for the horses and the steeds they brought to the place which was there, each according to his measure.”
n1100422: tn Heb “heart,” i.e., mind. (The Hebrew term translated “heart” often refers to the mental faculties.)
n1100423: tn Heb “the wisdom of Solomon was greater than the wisdom of all the sons of the east and all the wisdom of Egypt.”
n1100424: tn Heb “his name was in all the surrounding nations.”
n110051: sn The verse numbers in the English Bible differ from those in the Hebrew text (BHS) here; 5:1-18 in the English Bible corresponds to 5:15-32 in the Hebrew text. See the note at 4:21.
n110054: tn Heb “a house for the name of the Lord.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.
n110055: tn Heb “because of the battles which surrounded him until the Lord placed them under the soles of his feet.”
n110057: tn Heb “a house for the name of the Lord.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.
n110059: tn Or “Blessed be the Lord today, who….”
n1100511: tn Heb “I will satisfy all your desire with respect to cedar wood and with respect to the wood of evergreens.”
n1100512: tn Heb “I will place them [on? as?] rafts in the sea to the place where you designate to me.” This may mean he would send them by raft, or that he would tie them in raft-like bundles, and have ships tow them down to an Israelite port.
n1100514: tn Heb “as for you, you will satisfy my desire by giving food for my house.”
n1100515: tn Heb “and Hiram gave to Solomon cedar wood and the wood of evergreens, all his desire.”
n1100516: sn As a unit of dry measure a cor was roughly equivalent to six bushels.
n1100518:
tc The Hebrew text has “twenty cors,” but the ancient Greek version and the parallel text in 2 Chr 2:10 read “20,000 baths.”
sn A bath was a liquid measure roughly equivalent to six gallons (about 22 liters), so this was a quantity of about 120,000 gallons (440,000 liters).
n1100520: tn Heb “and Solomon supplied Hiram with 20,000 cors of wheat…pure olive oil. So Solomon would give to Hiram year by year.”
n1100521: tn Heb “a covenant,” referring to a formal peace treaty or alliance.
n1100523: sn Work crews. This Hebrew word (‏מַס‎, mas) refers to a group of laborers conscripted for royal or public service.
n1100527: tc Some Greek mss of the OT read “3,600”; cf. 2 Chr 2:2, 18 and NLT.
n1100528: tn Heb “besides thirty-three hundred from the officials of Solomon’s governors who were over the work, the ones ruling over the people, the ones doing the work.”
n1100531: tn Heb “the Gebalites.” The reading is problematic and some emend to a verb form meaning, “set the borders.”
n1100532: tc The LXX includes the words “for three years.”
n110061: sn During the month Ziv. This would be April-May, 966 b.c. by modern reckoning.
n110063: tn Heb “60 cubits.” A cubit was a unit of measure roughly equivalent to 18 inches or 45 cm. Measurements in vv. 2-10 have been converted to feet in the translation for clarity.
n110068: tn Heb “and he built on the wall of the temple an extension all around, the walls of the temple all around, for the main hall and for the holy place, and he made side rooms all around.”
n1100612: tn Or “offsets” (ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV); NIV “offset ledges.”
n1100613: tn Heb “so that [the beams] would not have a hold in the walls of the temple.”
n1100614: tn Heb “finished stone of the quarry,” i.e., stones chiseled and shaped at the time they were taken out of the quarry.
n1100615: tc The Hebrew text has “middle,” but the remainder of the verse suggests this is an error.
n1100616: tn Heb “by stairs they went up.” The word translated “stairs” occurs only here. Other options are “trapdoors” or “ladders.”
n1100617: tc The translation reads with a few medieval Hebrew mss, the Syriac Peshitta, and Vulgate ‏הַשְּׁלִשִׁית‎ (hashelishit, “the third”) rather than MT ‏הַשְּׁלִשִׁים‎ (hashelishim, “the thirty”).
n1100618: tn Heb “ built the house and completed it.”
n1100620: tn The word occurs only here; the precise meaning is uncertain.
n1100622: tn Heb “5 cubits.” This must refer to the height of each floor or room.
n1100626: tn Heb “and keep all my commandments by walking in them.”
n1100627: tn Heb “I will establish my word with you which I spoke to David your father.”
n1100628: tn Heb “ built the house and completed it.”
n1100629: tc The MT reads ‏קִירוֹת‎ (qirot, “walls”), but this should be emended to ‏קוֹרוֹת‎ (qorot, “rafters”). See BDB 900 s.v. ‏קוֹרָה‎.
n1100630: tn Heb “He built 20 cubits from the rear areas of the temple with cedar planks from the floor to the walls, and he built it on the inside for an inner sanctuary, for a holy place of holy places.”
n1100631: tc The MT reads ‏קִירוֹת‎ (qirot, “walls”), but this should be emended to ‏קוֹרוֹת‎ (qorot, “rafters”). See BDB 900 s.v. ‏קוֹרָה‎.
n1100632: tn Heb “and the house was 40 cubits, that is, the main hall before it.”
n1100633: tn Heb “Cedar was inside the temple, carvings of gourds (i.e., gourd-shaped ornaments) and opened flowers; the whole was cedar, no stone was seen.”
n1100634: tn Heb “20 cubits” (this measurement occurs three times in this verse).
n1100639: tn Heb “all the temple he plated with gold until all the temple was finished; and the whole altar which was in the inner sanctuary he plated with gold.”
n1100640: tn Heb “10 cubits” (a cubit was a unit of measure roughly equivalent to 18 inches or 45 cm).
n1100641: tn Heb “The first wing of the [one] cherub was 5 cubits, and the second wing of the cherub was 5 cubits, 10 cubits from the tips of his wings to the tips of his wings.”
n1100642: tn Heb “and the second cherub was 10 cubits, the two cherubim had one measurement and one shape.”
n1100643: tn Heb “the height of the first cherub was 10 cubits; and so was the second cherub.”
n1100644: tn Heb “in the midst of the inner house,” i.e., in the inner sanctuary.
n1100645: tn Heb “and their wings were in the middle of the room, touching wing to wing.”
n1100646: sn Inside and out probably refers to the inner and outer rooms within the building.
n1100648: sn Inside and out probably refers to the inner and outer rooms within the building.
n1100649: tn Heb “the pillar, doorposts, a fifth part” (the precise meaning of this description is uncertain).
n1100651: tn Heb “he plated [with] gold” (the precise object is not stated).
n1100652: tn Heb “and he hammered out the gold on the cherubim and the palm trees.”
n1100653: tn Heb “and so he did at the entrance of the main hall, doorposts of olive wood, from a fourth.”
n1100654: tn The words “he also made” are added for stylistic reasons.
n1100655: tc Heb “two of the leaves of the first door were folding, and two of the leaves of the second door were folding.” In the second half of the description, the MT has ‏קְלָעִים‎ (qelaʿim, “curtains”), but this probably should be emended to ‏צְלָעִים‎ (tselaʿim, “leaves”), which appears in the first half of the statement. One Hebrew ms, the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate support ‏צְלָעִים‎ (tselaʿim, “leaves”).
n1100656: sn In the month of Ziv. This would be April-May, 966 b.c. by modern reckoning.
n1100657: tn The words “of Solomon’s reign” are added for clarification. See v. 1.
n1100658: sn In the month Bul. This would be October-November 959 b.c. in modern reckoning.
n110071: tn Heb “His house Solomon built in thirteen years and he completed all his house.”
n110073: sn The Palace of the Lebanon Forest. This name was appropriate because of the large amount of cedar, undoubtedly brought from Lebanon, used in its construction. The cedar pillars in the palace must have given it the appearance of a forest.
n110077: tn Heb “and framed [windows in] three rows, and opening to opening three times.” The precise meaning of this description is uncertain. Another option might be, “overhung [in] three rows.” This might mean they were positioned high on the walls.
n110079: sn Rectangular in shape. That is, rather than arched.
n1100710: tn Heb “and all the entrances and the doorposts [had] four frames, and in front of opening to opening three times” (the precise meaning of the description is uncertain).
n1100714: tn Heb “and a porch was in front of them (i.e., the aforementioned pillars) and pillars and a roof in front of them (i.e., the aforementioned pillars and porch).” The precise meaning of the term translated “roof” is uncertain; it occurs only here and in Ezek 41:25-26.
n1100715: tn Heb “and a porch for the throne, where he was making judicial decisions, the Porch of Judgment, he made.”
n1100716: tc The Hebrew text reads, “from the floor to the floor.” The second occurrence of the term ‏הַקַּרְקָע‎ (haqqarqaʿ, “the floor”) is probably an error; one should emend to ‏הַקּוֹרוֹת‎ (haqqorot, “the rafters”). See 6:16.
n1100717: tn Heb “and his house where he lived, the other court [i.e., as opposed to the great court], separated from the house belonging to the hall, was like this work [i.e., this style of architecture].”
n1100718: tn Heb “and a house he was making for the daughter of Pharaoh, whom Solomon had taken, like this porch.”
n1100722: tn The precise meaning of the Hebrew word ‏טְפָחוֹת‎ (tefakhot) is uncertain, but it is clear that the referent stands in opposition to the foundation.
n1100723: tn Heb “stones of 10 cubits and stones of 8 cubits” (it is unclear exactly what dimension is being measured). If both numbers refer to the length of the stones (cf. NCV, CEV, NLT), then perhaps stones of two different sizes were used in some alternating pattern.
n1100728: tn Heb “King Solomon sent and took Hiram from Tyre.” In 2 Chr 2:13 (MT v. 12) and 4:11, 16 his name is spelled “Huram.”
n1100730: tn 2 Chr 2:14 (13 HT) says “from the daughters of Dan.”
n1100731: tn Heb “he was filled with the skill, understanding, and knowledge.”
n1100734: tn Heb “two capitals he made to place on the tops of the pillars, cast in bronze; 5 cubits was the height of the first capital, and 5 cubits was the height of the second capital.”
n1100735: tn Heb “there were seven for the first capital, and seven for the second capital.”
n1100736: tn Heb “he made the pillars, and two rows surrounding one latticework to cover the capitals which were on top of the pomegranates, and so he did for the second latticework.” The translation supplies “pomegranates” after “two rows,” and understands “pillars,” rather than “pomegranates,” to be the correct reading after “on top of.” The latter change finds support from many Hebrew mss and the ancient Greek version.
n1100737: tn Heb “the capitals which were on the top of the pillars were the work of lilies, in the porch, 4 cubits.” It is unclear exactly what dimension is being measured.
n1100738: tn Heb “and the capitals on the two pillars, also above, close beside the bulge which was beside the latticework, two hundred pomegranates in rows around, on the second capital.” The precise meaning of the word translated “bulge” is uncertain.
n1100740: tn The name “Yakin” appears to be a verbal form and probably means, “he establishes.”
n1100742: sn The meaning of the name Boaz is uncertain. For various proposals, see BDB 126-27 s.v. ‏בעז‎. One attractive option is to revocalize the name as ‏בְּעֹז‎ (beʿoz, “in strength”) and to understand it as completing the verbal form on the first pillar. Taking the words together and reading from right to left, one can translate the sentence, “he establishes [it] in strength.”
n1100743:
tn Heb “He made the sea, cast.”
sn This large basin was mounted on twelve bronze bulls and contained water for the priests to bathe themselves (2 Chr 4:6; cf. Exod 30:17-21).
n1100746: tn Heb “and a measuring line went around it 30 cubits all around.” The measurements are an approximation. The LXX has the number 33. Neither fit a strict calculation of the diameter time pi, but the precise details of measuring (e.g. the width of the lip or other factors) are not known and numbers may be rounded.
n1100748: tn Heb “10 cubits surrounding the sea all around.” The precise meaning of this description is uncertain.
n1100749: tn Heb “the gourd-shaped ornaments were in two rows, cast in its casting.”
n1100750: tn Heb “It.” The proper noun “The Sea” has been supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1100751: tn Heb “all their hindquarters were toward the inside.”
n1100752: tn Heb “2,000 baths” (a bath was a liquid measure roughly equivalent to six gallons).
n1100756: tn The precise meaning of these final words is uncertain. A possible literal translation would be, “wreaths, the work of descent.”
n1100757: tn Heb “and four its feet, supports to them.”
n1100758: tn The precise meaning of this last word, translated “wreaths,” is uncertain.
n1100759: tn Heb “And its opening from the inside to the top and upwards [was] a cubit, and its opening was round, the work of a stand, a cubit-and-a-half.” The precise meaning of this description is uncertain.
n1100760: tn Heb “also over its opening were carvings and their frames [were] squared, not round.”
n1100761: tn Heb “a cubit-and-a-half” (a cubit was a unit of measure roughly equivalent to 18 inches or 45 cm).
n1100762: tn Heb “four shoulders to the four sides of each stand, from the stand its shoulders.” The precise meaning of the description is uncertain.
n1100763: tn Heb “and on top of the stand, a half cubit [in] height, round all around” (the meaning of this description is uncertain).
n1100765: tn The precise meaning of this last word, translated “wreaths,” is uncertain.
n1100766: tn Heb “forty baths” (a bath was a liquid measure roughly equivalent to six gallons).
n1100767: tn Heb “4 cubits, each basin.” It is unclear which dimension is being measured.
n1100768: tn Heb “Hiram.” The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun (“he”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1100769: tn Heb “Hiram finished doing all the work which he did for King Solomon [on] the house of the Lord.”
n1100770: tn The words “he made” are added for stylistic reasons.
n1100772: tn Heb “which Hiram made for King Solomon [for] the house of the Lord.”
n1100774: tn Heb “Solomon left all the items, due to their very great abundance; the weight of the bronze was not sought.”
n1100775:
tn Heb “the bread of the face [or presence].” Many recent English versions employ “the bread of the Presence,” although this does not convey much to the modern reader.
sn This bread was viewed as a perpetual offering to God and was regarded as holy. See Lev 24:5-9.
n1100776: tn Heb “Solomon.” The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun (“he”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n110081: tc The Old Greek translation includes the following words at the beginning of ch. 8: “It so happened that when Solomon finished building the Lord’s temple and his own house, after twenty years.”
n110083: tn Heb “Then Solomon convened the elders of Israel, the heads of the tribes, the chiefs of the fathers belonging to the sons of Israel to King Solomon [in] Jerusalem to bring up the ark of the covenant of the Lord from the City of David (it is Zion).”
n110084: sn The festival. This was the Feast of Tabernacles, see Lev 23:34.
n110085: sn The month Ethanim. This would be September-October in modern reckoning.
n110086:
tn Heb “the tent of assembly.”
sn The tent of meeting. See Exod 33:7-11.
n110087: tn Heb “and they carried the ark of the Lord…. The priests and the Levites carried them.”
n110088: tn Heb “And King Solomon and all the assembly of Israel, those who had been gathered to him, [were] before the ark, sacrificing sheep and cattle which could not be counted or numbered because of the abundance.”
n110089: tn The word “assigned” is supplied in the translation for clarification.
n1100810: sn And its poles. These poles were used to carry the ark. See Exod 25:13-15.
n1100812: sn Horeb is another name for Mount Sinai.
n1100814: tn Heb “were not able to stand to serve.”
n1100816: tn The words “O Lord” do not appear in the original text, but they are supplied for clarification; Solomon addresses the Lord in prayer at this point.
n1100818: tn Heb “and he blessed all the assembly of Israel, and all the assembly of Israel was standing.”
n1100819: tn The Hebrew text reads, “by his hand he has fulfilled.”
n1100820: tn The Hebrew text reads, “he promised by his mouth.”
n1100821: tn Heb “saying.” The word is carried over from the end of verse 15.
n1100822:
tn Heb “to build a house for my name to be there.”
sn To build a temple in which to live (Heb “to build a house for my name to be there”). In the OT, the word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.
n1100823: tn Heb “and it was with the heart of David my father.”
n1100824: tn Heb “to build a house for the name of the Lord God of Israel.” The word “name” in the OT sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.
n1100825: tn Heb “Because it was with your heart to build a house for my name, you did well that it was with your heart.”
n1100826: tn Heb “your son, the one who came out of your body, he will build the temple for my name.”
n1100832: tn Heb “one who keeps the covenant and the loyal love.” The expression is a hendiadys.
n1100833: tn Heb “who walk before you with all their heart.”
n1100834: tn Heb “[you] who kept to your servant David my father that which you spoke to him.”
n1100835: tn Heb “you spoke by your mouth and by your hand you fulfilled, as this day.”
n1100836: tn Heb “there will not be cut off from you a man from before me sitting on the throne of Israel.”
n1100837: tn Heb “watch their way.” The Hebrew and English colloquialisms are similar. The related ideas “way” and “steps” represent behavior in a broad sense in each language.
n1100838: tn Heb “guard their way by walking before me as you have walked before me.”
n1100841: tn Heb “Indeed, can God really live on the earth?” The rhetorical question expects the answer, “Of course not,” the force of which the translation above seeks to reflect.
n1100846: tn Heb “so your eyes might be open toward this house night and day, toward the place about which you said, ‘My name will be there.’”
n1100847: tn Heb “by listening to the prayer which your servant is praying concerning this place.”
n1100848: tn Heb “listen to the request of your servant and your people Israel which they are praying concerning this place.”
n1100849: tn Heb “and you, hear inside your dwelling place, inside heaven.” The precise nuance of the preposition ‏אֶל‎ (ʾel), used here with the verb “hear,” is unclear. One expects the preposition “from,” which appears in the parallel text in 2 Chr 6:21. The nuance “inside; among” is attested for ‏אֶל‎ (see Gen 23:19; 1 Sam 10:22; Jer 4:3), but in each case a verb of motion is employed with the preposition, unlike 1 Kgs 8:30. The translation above (“from inside”) is based on the demands of the immediate context rather than attested usage elsewhere.
n1100851:
tn Heb “and forgive the man who sins against his neighbor when one takes up against him a curse to curse him and the curse comes before your altar in this house.” In the Hebrew text the words “and forgive” conclude v. 30, but the accusative sign at the beginning of v. 31 suggests the verb actually goes with what follows in v. 31. The parallel text in 2 Chr 6:22 begins with “and if,” rather than the accusative sign. In this case “forgive” must be taken with what precedes, and v. 31 must be taken as the protasis (“if” clause) of a conditional sentence, with v. 32 being the apodosis (“then” clause) that completes the sentence.
sn Be willing to forgive the accused if the accusation is false. At first it appears that Solomon is asking God to forgive the guilty party. But in v. 32 Solomon asks the Lord to discern who is guilty and innocent, so v. 31 must refer to a situation where an accusation has been made, but not yet proven. The very periphrastic translation reflects this interpretation.
n1100852: tn Heb “and you, hear [from] heaven and act and judge your servants by declaring the guilty to be guilty, to give his way on his head, and to declare the innocent to be innocent, to give to him according to his innocence.”
n1100853: tn Heb “when.” In the Hebrew text vv. 33-34 actually contain one lengthy conditional sentence, which the translation has divided into two sentences for stylistic reasons.
n1100854: tn Or “are struck down before an enemy.”
n1100856: tn Heb “and they pray and ask for help.”
n1100857: tn Heb “when.” In the Hebrew text vv. 35-36a actually contain one lengthy conditional sentence, which the translation has divided into two sentences for stylistic reasons.
n1100858: tn Heb “they”; the referent (your people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1100860: tn The Hebrew text has “because you answer them,” as if the verb is from ‏עָנָה‎ (ʿanah, “to answer”). However, this reference to a divine answer is premature, since the next verse asks for God to intervene in mercy. It is better to revocalize the consonantal text as ‏תְעַנֵּם‎ (teʿannem, “you afflict them”), a Piel verb form from the homonym ‏עָנָה‎ (ʿanah, “to afflict”).
n1100861: tn The translation understands ‏כִּי‎ (ki) in an emphatic or asseverative sense.
n1100862: tn Heb “the good way in which they should walk.”
n1100864: tn Actually two Hebrew terms appear here, both of which are usually taken as referring to locusts. Perhaps different stages of growth or different varieties are in view.
n1100866: tn Heb “every prayer, every request for help which will be to all the people, to all your people Israel.”
n1100867: tn Heb “which they know, each the pain of his heart.”
n1100868: tn The words “their sin” are added for clarification.
n1100869: tn Heb “and act and give to each one according to all his ways because you know his heart.” In the Hebrew text vv. 37-39a actually contain one lengthy conditional sentence, which the translation has divided up for stylistic reasons.
n1100870: tn Heb “Indeed you know, you alone, the heart of all the sons of mankind.”
n1100873: tn Heb “your name.” In the OT the word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor. The “name” of the Lord sometimes designates the Lord himself, being indistinguishable from the proper name.
n1100874: tn Heb “your great name.” See the note on the word “reputation” in the previous verse.
n1100875: tn Heb “and your strong hand and your outstretched arm.”
n1100876: tn Heb “and do all which the foreigner calls to [i.e., “requests of”] you.”
n1100877: tn Heb “your name.” See the note on the word “reputation” in v. 41.
n1100879: tn Heb “that your name is called over this house which I built.” The Hebrew idiom “to call the name over” indicates ownership. See 2 Sam 12:28.
n1100880: tn Heb “When your people go out for battle against their enemies in the way which you send them.”
n1100881: tn Or perhaps “to you, O Lord.” See 2 Chr 6:34.
n1100882: tn Heb “your name.” See the note on the word “reputation” in v. 41.
n1100883: tn Heb “their prayer and their request for help.”
n1100885: tn Heb “they”; the referent (your people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1100887: tn Heb “they”; the referent (your people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1100888: tn Or “stop and reflect”; Heb “bring back to their heart.”
n1100892: tn Heb “your name.” See the note on the word “reputation” in v. 41.
n1100893: tn Heb “their prayer and their request for help.”
n1100895: tn Heb “and forgive your people who have sinned against you, and all their rebellious acts by which they rebelled against you, and grant them mercy before their captors so they will show them mercy.”
n1100898:
tn The Hebrew term ‏כּוּר‎ (kur, “furnace,” cf. Akkadian kūru) is a metaphor for the intense heat of purification. A ‏כּוּר‎ was not a source of heat but a crucible (“iron-smelting furnace”) in which precious metals were melted down and their impurities burned away (see I. Cornelius, NIDOTTE 2:618-19). Thus Egypt served not as a place of punishment for the Israelites, but as a place of refinement to bring Israel to a place of submission to divine sovereignty.
sn From the middle of the iron-smelting furnace. The metaphor of a furnace suggests fire and heat and is an apt image to remind the people of the suffering they endured while slaves in Egypt.
n11008100: tn Heb “to listen to them in all their calling out to you.”
n11008104: tn Heb “he has given a resting place to his people Israel.”
n11008105: tn Heb “not one word from his entire good word he spoke by Moses his servant has fallen.”
n11008106: tn Heb “to bend our hearts toward him.” The infinitive is subordinate to the initial prayer, “may the Lord our God be with us.” The Hebrew term ‏לֵבָב‎ (levav, “heart”) here refers to the people’s volition and will.
n11008109: tn Heb “May these words of mine, which I have requested before the Lord, be near the Lord our God day and night.”
n11008112: tn Heb “the Lord, he is the God, there is no other.”
n11008113: tn Heb “may your hearts be complete with the Lord our God.”
n11008117: tn Heb “peace offerings that he sacrificed.” “Peace offerings” could be “tokens of peace”; NIV, TEV “fellowship offerings.”
n11008118: tn Heb “to hold the burnt sacrifices, grain offerings, and the fat of the peace offerings.”
n11008119: tn Or “the Wadi of Egypt” (NAB, NIV, NRSV); CEV “the Egyptian Gorge.”
n11008120: tn Heb “Solomon held at that time the festival, and all Israel was with him, a great assembly from Lebo Hamath to the Stream of Egypt, before the Lord our God for seven days and seven days, fourteen days.”
n11008121: tn Heb “on the eighth day” (that is, the day after the second seven-day sequence).
n110091: tn Heb “and all the desire of Solomon which he wanted to do.”
n110092: sn In the same way he had appeared to him at Gibeon. See 1 Kgs 3:5.
n110094: tn Heb “by placing my name there perpetually” (or perhaps, “forever”).
n110095: tn Heb “and my eyes and my heart will be there all the days.”
n110096: tn Heb “As for you, if you walk before me, as David your father walked, in integrity of heart and in uprightness, by doing all which I commanded you, [and] you keep my rules and my regulations.” Verse 4 is actually a lengthy protasis (“if” section) of a conditional sentence, the apodosis (“then” section) of which appears in v. 5.
n110097: tn Heb “I will establish the throne of your kingdom over Israel forever.”
n110098: tn Heb “there will not be cut off from you a man from upon the throne of Israel.”
n1100910: tn Heb “and walk and serve other gods and bow down to them.”
n1100911: tn Heb “I will cut off Israel from upon the surface of the land.”
n1100912:
tn Heb “and the temple which I consecrated for my name I will send away from before my face.”
sn Instead of “I will send away,” the parallel text in 2 Chr 7:20 has “I will throw away.” The two verbs sound very similar in Hebrew, so the discrepancy is likely due to an oral transmissional error.
n1100913: tn Heb “will become a proverb and a taunt,” that is, a proverbial example of destruction and an object of reproach.
n1100914: tn Heb “and this house will be high [or elevated].” The statement makes little sense in this context, which predicts the desolation that judgment will bring. Some treat the clause as concessive, “Even though this temple is lofty [now].” Others, following the lead of several ancient versions, emend the text to, “this temple will become a heap of ruins.”
n1100915: tn Heb “hiss,” or perhaps “whistle.” This refers to a derisive sound one would make when taunting an object of ridicule.
n1100918: tn Heb “and they took hold of other gods and bowed down to them and served them.”
n1100919: tn Heb “the two houses, the house of the Lord and the house of the king.”
n1100921: tn Heb “they were not agreeable in his eyes.”
n1100923: tn Heb “my brother.” Kings allied through a parity treaty would sometimes address each other as “my brother.” See 1 Kgs 20:32-33.
n1100924: tn Heb “he called them the land of Cabul to this day.” The significance of the name is unclear, though it appears to be disparaging. The name may be derived from a root, attested in Akkadian and Arabic, meaning “bound” or “restricted.” Some propose a wordplay, pointing out that the name “Cabul” sounds like a Hebrew phrase meaning, “like not,” or “as good as nothing.”
n1100925: tn The Hebrew term ‏כִּכָּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or to a standard unit of weight, generally regarded as a talent. Since the accepted weight for a talent of metal is about 75 pounds, this would have amounted to about 9,000 pounds of gold (cf. NCV, NLT); CEV “five tons”; TEV “4,000 kilogrammes.”
n1100926: sn The work crews. This Hebrew word ‏מַס‎ (mas) refers to a group of laborers conscripted for royal or public service.
n1100929: tn The words “the cities of” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n1100932: tn The Hebrew text has “in the wilderness, in the land.”
n1100933: tn Heb “to Solomon.” The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun (“him”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1100934: tn Heb “the cities of the chariots and the cities of the horses.”
n1100935: tn Heb “and the desire of Solomon which he desired to build in Jerusalem and in Lebanon and in all the land of his kingdom.”
n1100936: tn Heb “all the people who were left from the Amorites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites, who were not from the sons of Israel.”
n1100937: tn Heb “their sons who were left after them in the land, whom the sons of Israel were unable to wipe out, and Solomon raised them up for a crew of labor to this day.”
n1100938: sn These work crews. The work crews referred to here must be different than the temporary crews described in 5:13-16.
n1100939: tn Heb “officers of his chariots and his horses.”
n1100940: tn Heb “these [were] the officials of the governors who were over the work belonging to Solomon, five hundred fifty, the ones ruling over the people, the ones doing the work.”
n1100941: sn The phrase City of David refers here to the fortress of Zion in Jerusalem, not to Bethlehem. See 2 Sam 5:7.
n1100942: tn Heb “As soon as Pharaoh’s daughter went up from the City of David to her house which he built for her, then he built the terrace.”
n1100943: tn Or “tokens of peace”; NIV, TEV “fellowship offerings.”
n1100945: tn Or “a fleet” (in which case “ships” would be implied).
n1100946: tn Heb “and Hiram sent with the fleet his servants, men of ships, [who] know the sea, [to be] with the servants of Solomon.”
n1100948: tn The Hebrew term ‏כִּכָּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or to a standard unit of weight, generally regarded as a talent. Since the accepted weight for a talent of metal is about 75 pounds, this would have amounted to about 31,500 pounds of gold (cf. NCV); CEV, NLT “sixteen tons”; TEV “more than 14,000 kilogrammes.”
n110101:
tn Heb “the report about Solomon.”
tc The Hebrew text also has, “to the name of the Lord,” which is very awkward due to its unusual syntax. The phrase is omitted in the parallel passage in 2 Chr 9:1. The word “report” is followed by the preposition ‏ל‎ (lamed) in Isa 23:5 and Hos 7:12 and indicates whom the message came to. And otherwise the collocation of ‏לְשֵׁם‎ (leshem, “to the name”) does not follow either a proper noun or the word report elsewhere in scripture. If retained, perhaps it should be translated, “for the reputation of the Lord.”
n110105: tn Heb “with very great strength.” The Hebrew term ‏חַיִל‎ (khayil, “strength”) may refer here to the size of her retinue (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV) or to the great wealth she brought with her.
n110107: tn Heb “Solomon declared to her all her words; there was not a word hidden from the king which he did not declare to her.” If riddles are specifically in view (see v. 1), then one might translate, “Solomon explained to her all her riddles; there was no riddle too complex for the king.”
n1101011: tn Heb “the seating of his servants and the standing of his attendants.”
n1101012: tn Heb “there was no breath still in her.”
n1101013: tn Heb “about your words [or perhaps, “deeds”] and your wisdom.”
n1101014: tn Heb “the half was not told to me.”
n1101016: tn Heb “How happy are your men! How happy are these servants of yours, who stand before you continually, who hear your wisdom!”
n1101019: tn The Hebrew term ‏כִּכָּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or to a standard unit of weight, generally regarded as a talent. Since the accepted weight for a talent of metal is about 75 pounds, this would have amounted to about 9,000 pounds of gold (cf. NCV, NLT); CEV “five tons”; TEV “4,000 kilogrammes.”
n1101020: tn Heb “there has not come like those spices yet for quantity which the queen of Sheba gave to King Solomon.”
n1101021: tn This Hebrew architectural term occurs only here. The meaning is uncertain; some have suggested “banisters” or “parapets”; cf. TEV, NLT “railings.” The parallel passage in 2 Chr 9:11 has a different word, meaning “tracks,” or perhaps “steps.”
n1101022: tn Two types of stringed instruments are specifically mentioned, the ‏כִּנּוֹר‎ (kinnor, “zither” [?]), and ‏נֶבֶל‎ (nevel, “harp”).
n1101023: tn Heb “there has not come thus, the fine timber, and there has not been seen to this day.”
n1101024: tn Heb “besides what he had given her according to the hand of King Solomon.”
n1101026: tn The Hebrew term ‏כִּכָּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or to a standard unit of weight, generally regarded as a talent. Since the accepted weight for a talent of metal is about 75 pounds, this would have amounted to about 50,000 pounds of gold (cf. NCV); CEV, NLT “twenty-five tons”; TEV “almost 23,000 kilogrammes.”
n1101027: tn Heb “the weight of the gold which came to Solomon in one year was 666 talents of gold.”
n1101029: tn The Hebrew text has simply “six hundred,” with no unit of measure given.
n1101030: sn Three minas. The mina was a unit of measure for weight.
n1101031: sn The Palace of the Lebanon Forest. This name was appropriate because of the large amount of cedar, undoubtedly brought from Lebanon, used in its construction. The cedar pillars in the palace must have given it the appearance of a forest.
n1101032: tn Heb “[There were] armrests on each side of the place of the seat, and two lions standing beside the armrests.”
n1101033: tn Heb “nothing like it had been made for all the kingdoms.”
n1101034: tn Heb “there was no silver, it was not regarded as anything in the days of Solomon.”
n1101035: tn Heb “a fleet of Tarshish [ships].” This probably refers to large ships either made in or capable of traveling to the distant western port of Tarshish.
n1101038: tn The meaning of this word is unclear. Some suggest “baboons.”
n1101039: tn Heb “King Solomon was greater than all the kings of the earth with respect to wealth and with respect to wisdom.”
n1101040: tc The Old Greek translation and Syriac Peshitta have “all the kings of the earth.” See 2 Chr 9:23.
n1101041: tn Heb “and all the earth was seeking the face of Solomon to hear his wisdom which God had placed in his heart.”
n1101042: tn Heb “and they were bringing each one his gift, items of silver…and mules, the matter of a year in a year.”
n1101044: tn Heb “he placed them in the chariot cities and with the king in Jerusalem.”

n1101049: tn Heb “and a chariot went up and came out of Egypt for six hundred silver [pieces], and a horse for one hundred fifty, and in the same way to all the kings of the Hittites and to the kings of Aram by their hand they brought out.”

n110113: tn Heb “Solomon clung to them for love.” The pronominal suffix, translated “them,” is masculine here, even though it appears the foreign women are in view. Perhaps this is due to attraction to the masculine forms used of the nations earlier in the verse.
n110115: sn Concubines were slave women in ancient Near Eastern societies who were the legal property of their master, but who could have legitimate sexual relations with their master. A concubine’s status was more elevated than a mere servant, but she was not free and did not have the legal rights of a free wife. The children of a concubine could, in some instances, become equal heirs with the children of the free wife. The usage in the present passage suggests that after the period of the Judges concubines may have become more of a royal prerogative (cf. also 2 Sam 21:10-14).
n110118: tn Heb “his heart was not complete with the Lord his God, like the heart of David his father.”

n1101138: tn Heb “Indeed what do you lack with me, that now you are seeking to go to your land?”
n1101140: sn So Hadad asked Pharaoh…. This lengthy description of Hadad’s exile in Egypt explains why Hadad wanted to oppose Solomon and supports the author’s thesis that his hostility to Solomon found its ultimate source in divine providence. Though Hadad enjoyed a comfortable life in Egypt, when the Lord raised him up (apparently stirring up his desire for vengeance) he decided to leave the comforts of Egypt and return to Edom.

n1101146: tn Heb “Ephrathite,” which here refers to an Ephraimite (see HALOT 81 s.v. ‏אֶפְרַיִם‎).

n110122: tn Heb “him”; the referent (Rehoboam) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

n110124: tn Heb “and Jeroboam lived in Egypt.” The parallel text in 2 Chr 10:2 reads, “and Jeroboam returned from Egypt.” In a purely consonantal text the forms “and he lived” and “and he returned” are identical (‏וישׁב‎).
n110127: tn Heb “but you, now, lighten the burdensome work of your father and the heavy yoke which he placed on us, and we will serve you.” In the Hebrew text the prefixed verbal form with vav (‏וְנַעַבְדֶךָ‎, [venaʿavdekha] “and we will serve you”) following the imperative (‏הָקֵל‎ [haqel], “lighten”) indicates purpose (or result). The conditional sentence used in the translation above is an attempt to bring out the logical relationship between these forms.
n1101210: tn Heb “and serve them and answer them,” understood as “serve them in how you answer them,” hence “grant their request.”
n1101212: tn Heb “all the days.” The Hebrew phrase contrasts what he is asked to do “today” (literally “the day”) with the benefit for “all the days.”
n1101213: tn Heb “He rejected the advice of the elders which they advised and he consulted the young men with whom he had grown up, who stood before him.” The referent (Rehoboam) of the initial pronoun (“he”) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1101214: tn In the Hebrew text the verb “we will respond” is plural, although it can be understood as an editorial “we.” The ancient versions have the singular here.
n1101215: tn Heb “Lighten the yoke which your father placed on us.”

n1101219: tn Heb “and now my father placed upon you a heavy yoke, but I will add to your yoke.”
n1101220: tn Heb “My father punished you with whips, but I will punish you with scorpions.” “Scorpions” might allude to some type of torture using poisonous insects, but more likely it refers to a type of whip that inflicts an especially biting, painful wound. n1101223: tn Heb “My father made your yoke heavy, but I will add to your yoke.”

n1101310: tn Heb “appease” or “soften the face of,” twice in this verse.

n1101316: tn Heb “the words which he had spoken to the king, and they told them to their father.”
n1101317: tc The MT reads ‏וַיִּרְאוּ‎ (vayyirʾu, “they saw”) the Qal preterite of ‏רָאָה‎ (raʾah, “to see”). Some translations render this as pluperfect “they had seen” (KJV, NASB), but then the verb should have been preceded by a different construction. Other translations (NIV, ESV, NRSV) follow some ancient versions and emend the verbal form to a Hiphil with pronominal suffix ‏וַיַּרְאֻהוּ‎ (vayyarʾuhu, “and they showed him”).
n1101318: tn Heb “I am unable to return with you or to go with you.”

The old man’s response to the prophet’s death (see vv. 26-32) suggests he did not trick him with malicious intent. In the Hebrew text vv. 21-22 are one long sentence comprised of a causal clause giving the reason for divine punishment (vv. 21-22a) and the main clause announcing the punishment (v. 22b). The translation divides this lengthy sentence for stylistic reasons.

n1101326: tn Heb “will not come to the tomb of your fathers.”

n1101341: tn Heb “did not turn from his evil way.”

n110147:
tn Heb “like this and like this.”
sn Tell her such and such. Certainly the Lord gave Ahijah a specific message to give to Jeroboam’s wife (see vv. 6-16), but the author of Kings here condenses the Lord’s message with the words “so-and-so.” For dramatic effect he prefers to have us hear the message from Ahijah’s lips as he speaks to the king’s wife.
n110148: tn Heb “I am sent to you [with] a hard [message].”
n110149: tn The Hebrew text has “because” at the beginning of the sentence. In the Hebrew text vv. 7-11 are one long sentence comprised of a causal clause giving the reason for divine punishment (vv. 7-9) and the main clause announcing the punishment (vv. 10-11). The translation divides this lengthy sentence for stylistic reasons.
n1101411: tn Heb “you went and you made for yourself other gods, and metal [ones], angering me, and you threw me behind your back.”
n1101412: sn Disaster. There is a wordplay in the Hebrew text. The word translated “disaster” (‏רָעָה‎, raʿah) is from the same root as the expression “you have sinned” in v. 9 (‏וַתָּרַע‎ [vattaraʿ], from ‏רָעַע‎, [raʿaʿ]). Jeroboam’s sins would receive an appropriate punishment.
n1101414: tn Heb “and I will cut off from Jeroboam those who urinate against a wall (including both those who are) restrained and let free (or “abandoned”) in Israel.” The precise meaning of the idiomatic phrase ‏עָצוּר וְעָזוּב‎ (ʿatsur veʿazuv) is uncertain. For various options see HALOT 871 s.v. ‏עצר‎ 6 and M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 107. The two terms are usually taken as polar opposites (“slaves and freemen” or “minors and adults”), but Cogan and Tadmor, on the basis of contextual considerations (note the usage with ‏אֶפֶס‎ [ʾefes], “nothing but”) in Deut 32:36 and 2 Kgs 14:26, argue convincingly that the terms are synonyms, meaning “restrained and abandoned,” and refer to incapable or incapacitated individuals.
n1101415: tn The traditional view understands the verb ‏בָּעַר‎ (baʿar) to mean “burn.” Manure was sometimes used as fuel (see Ezek 4:12, 15). However, an alternate view takes ‏בָּעַר‎ as a homonym meaning “sweep away” (HALOT 146 s.v. II ‏בער‎). In this case one might translate, “I will sweep away the dynasty of Jeroboam, just as one sweeps away manure it is gone” (cf. ASV, NASB, TEV). Either metaphor emphasizes the thorough and destructive nature of the coming judgment.
n1101416: tn The Hebrew text has “belonging to Jeroboam” here.
n1101419: tn Heb “This is the day. What also now?” The precise meaning of the second half of the statement is uncertain.
n1101420: tn The elliptical Hebrew text reads literally “and the Lord will strike Israel as a reed sways in the water.”
n1101422: tn Heb “the River.” In biblical Hebrew this is a typical reference to the Euphrates River. The name “Euphrates” has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1101423:
tn Heb “because they made their Asherah poles that anger the Lord”; or “their images of Asherah”; ASV, NASB “their Asherim”; NCV “they set up idols to worship Asherah.”
sn Asherah was a leading deity of the Canaanite pantheon, wife/sister of El and goddess of fertility. She was commonly worshiped at shrines in or near groves of evergreen trees, or, failing that, at places marked by wooden poles. These were to be burned or cut down (Deut 12:3; 16:21; Judg 6:25, 28, 30; 2 Kgs 18:4).
n1101426: tn Heb “As for the rest of the events of Jeroboam, how he fought and how he ruled, are they not written on the scroll of the events of the days of the kings of Israel?”

n1101519: tn Heb “what was right in the eyes of the Lord.”
n1101520: tn Heb “father,” but Hebrew often uses the terms “father” and “mother” for grandparents and more remote ancestors.
n1101521: tn The word used here, ‏גִלּוּלִים‎ [gillulim], is always used as a disdainful reference to idols. It is generally thought to have originally referred to “dung pellets” (cf. KBL 183 s.v. ‏גִלּוּלִים‎). It is only one of several terms used in this way, such as “worthless things” (‏אֱלִילִים‎, ʾelilim), “vanities” or “empty winds” (‏הֲבָלִים‎, havalim).
n1101523: tn Heb “mother,” but Hebrew often uses the terms “father” and “mother” for grandparents and more remote ancestors.
n1101524: tn The Hebrew term ‏גְּבִירָה‎ (gevirah) can denote “queen” or “queen mother” depending on the context. Here the latter is indicated, since Maacah was the wife of Rehoboam and mother of Abijah.

n1101717:
tn Heb “is truly in your mouth.”
sn This episode is especially significant in light of Ahab’s decision to promote Baal worship in Israel. In Canaanite mythology the drought that swept over the region (v. 1) would signal that Baal, a fertility god responsible for providing food for his subjects, had been defeated by the god of death and was imprisoned in the underworld. While Baal was overcome by death and unable to function like a king, Israel’s God demonstrated his sovereignty and superiority to death by providing food for a widow and restoring life to her son. And he did it all in Sidonian territory, Baal’s back yard, as it were. The episode demonstrates that Israel’s God, not Baal, is the true king who provides food and controls life and death. This polemic against Baalism reaches its climax in the next chapter, when the Lord proves that he, not Baal, controls the elements of the storm and determines when the rains will fall.

The two terms are usually taken as polar opposites (“slaves and freemen” or “minors and adults”), but Cogan and Tadmor, on the basis of contextual considerations (note the usage with ‏אֶפֶס‎ (ʾefes), “nothing but”) in Deut 32:36 and 2 Kgs 14:26, argue convincingly that the terms are synonyms, meaning “restrained and abandoned,” and refer to incapable or incapacitated individuals.

n1102215: sn “Attack! You will succeed; the Lord will hand it over to the king.” One does not expect Micaiah, having just vowed to speak only what the Lord tells him, to agree with the other prophets and give the king an inaccurate prophecy. Micaiah’s actions became understandable later, when it is revealed that the Lord desires to deceive the king and lead him to his demise. The Lord even dispatches a lying spirit to deceive Ahab’s prophets. Micaiah can lie to the king because he realizes this lie is from the Lord. It is important to note that in v. 14 Micaiah only vows to speak the word of the Lord; he does not necessarily say he will tell the truth. In this case the Lord’s word itself is deceptive. Only when the king adjures him to tell the truth (v. 16), does Micaiah do so.

n1102220: tn Heb “the spirit.” The significance of the article prefixed to ‏רוּחַ‎ (ruakh) is uncertain, but it could contain a clue as to this spirit’s identity, especially when interpreted in light of v. 24. It is certainly possible, and probably even likely, that the article is used in a generic or dramatic sense and should be translated, “a spirit.” In the latter case it would show that this spirit was vivid and definite in the mind of Micaiah the storyteller. However, if one insists that the article indicates a well-known or universally known spirit, the following context provides a likely referent. Verse 24 tells how Zedekiah slapped Micaiah in the face and then asked sarcastically, “Which way did the spirit from the Lord (‏רוּחַ־יְהוָה‎, [ruakh Yahweh], Heb “the spirit of the Lord”) go when he went from me to speak to you?” When the phrase “the spirit of the Lord” refers to the divine spirit (rather than the divine breath or mind, Isa 40:7, 13) elsewhere, the spirit energizes an individual or group for special tasks or moves one to prophesy. This raises the possibility that the deceiving spirit of vv. 20-23 is the same as the divine spirit mentioned by Zedekiah in v. 24. This would explain why the article is used on ‏רוּחַ‎; he can be called “the spirit” because he is the well-known spirit who energizes the prophets.

n1102222: tn The Hebrew text has two imperfects connected by ‏וְגַם‎ (vegam). These verbs could be translated as specific futures, “you will deceive and also you will prevail,” in which case the Lord is assuring the spirit of success on his mission. However, in a commissioning context (note the following imperatives) such as this, it is more likely that the imperfects are injunctive, in which case one could translate, “Deceive, and also overpower.”
